summary: Joel Spolsky argues that software companies should maintain radio silence about products under development rather than pre-announcing features. He contrasts Apple's surprise-driven launches with Microsoft's years-long public development cycles, advocating strongly for the Apple approach. He outlines five reasons for keeping quiet: avoiding broken promises, denying competitors advance notice, managing customer expectations, preserving flexibility, and keeping the policy simple enough for all employees to follow. He dismisses the value of advance buzz by pointing to the Segway's wasted early publicity and argues that early feedback can be gathered from a small group of selected customers rather than the entire world. Spolsky acknowledges that this policy may cost occasional sales but considers it worthwhile to protect reputation and avoid the vaporware trap.
key_points: Pre-announcing features gives competitors a free roadmap and at least a six-month head start on matching your innovations
Customers will always imagine announced features doing far more than intended, guaranteeing disappointment upon delivery
Publicity that drops when nobody can actually buy the product is roughly 90% wasted, as the Segway launch demonstrated
A simple 'radio silence' policy is enforceable across an entire company, while nuanced disclosure policies inevitably lead to leaks
Early feedback can be gathered effectively from a few hundred carefully selected customers rather than opening the floodgates to the entire world
money_quotes: Every word I say, by definition, is a promise.
The best way to avoid breaking promises is not to make any.
If you keep your mouth shut until you ship, you will be guaranteed at least half a year without competition before everyone else matches that cool new feature.

When you tell someone about the upcoming clam steaming feature, no matter how careful you are to delimit what it actually does, they will inevitably spin elaborate fantasies about how it cures baldness and warts and has a telepathic user interface.
I'm inclined to think that publicity that comes out when you can't actually buy the product is 90% wasted.
If your policy is Radio Silence, every employee understands it and can follow it. If your policy is in any way complicated, nobody is sure what to do and things leak.
It's better than selling you something that doesn't fit your needs and having you get pissed off at me, or getting a reputation as being unable to deliver.
key_insight: Software companies should default to complete silence about unreleased products because the costs of pre-announcing — broken promises, competitive exposure, inflated expectations, and lost flexibility — far outweigh the marginal benefits of advance buzz.
